title: Central Idea
description: Assessment of the concept that the central idea is what the text is mostly saying, supported across the whole text.

questions.prompt: What is the best definition of central idea?

choices.text: A minor fact that appears in one paragraph
choices.feedback: A central idea is broader than one small fact. It is supported across the text.

choices.text: What the entire text is mostly saying
choices.feedback: This is correct because the central idea is the main message developed across the whole text.

choices.text: The most interesting opinion a reader has after reading
choices.feedback: A central idea comes from the text itself, not from a reader's personal opinion.

choices.text: A title chosen to make the text sound dramatic
choices.feedback: A title may hint at meaning, but it is not the same as the central idea.

questions.prompt: An article explains that students perform better when they sleep enough, gives research on attention and memory, and describes school start-time debates. Which statement is the best central idea?

choices.text: Some schools begin classes earlier than others.
choices.feedback: This is a supporting detail, not the main idea developed across the article.

choices.text: Sleep affects student learning, so school schedules matter.
choices.feedback: This is correct because it captures the main point supported by the research and discussion in the article.

choices.text: Memory is an important brain function.
choices.feedback: This is too narrow because it focuses on one detail instead of the overall message.

choices.text: Students should always go to bed at the same time.
choices.feedback: This adds advice that may not be the main point the article develops.

questions.prompt: A student says the central idea of a text is "Paragraph 2 explains one cause of the problem." Why is that response weak?

choices.text: It focuses on one section instead of the meaning of the whole text
choices.feedback: This is correct because a central idea must represent the text as a whole, not just one paragraph.

choices.text: It uses too many words to be a central idea
choices.feedback: Length is not the main issue. The problem is that the response is too limited in scope.

choices.text: It should include the author's exact wording only
choices.feedback: A central idea can be stated in your own words as long as it accurately reflects the text.

choices.text: It must mention every supporting detail in the article
choices.feedback: A strong central idea does not list every detail; it captures the overall message.

questions.prompt: Which question would best help a reader identify a central idea while reading?

choices.text: Which sentence has the most difficult vocabulary word?
choices.feedback: This question focuses on vocabulary, not the overall meaning of the text.

choices.text: What is the author mostly trying to say across the entire text?
choices.feedback: This is correct because central idea depends on the main message developed throughout the text.
Paragraph length does not help identify the central idea.

choices.text: What punctuation mark appears most often?
choices.feedback: Punctuation frequency does not determine the text's central idea.

questions.prompt: True or False: A central idea should be supported by multiple details across the text, not by a single isolated fact.

choices.feedback: This is correct because a central idea is developed and supported across the text as a whole.
This is incorrect because one isolated fact is not enough to establish a central idea.
